Disable Sync Computed Embeddings for Column A; enable embedding sync for Column B to speed up joins.
Enable Sync Computed Embeddings for both Column A and Column B so both are searchable by similarity.
Disable Sync Computed Embeddings for Column A and Column B to guarantee embeddings are always freshly computed.
questions.explanation: Column A is the semantic text field, so syncing its computed embeddings stores vectors in the index and avoids recomputation on subsequent queries. Column B is an identifier/return value and should be stored as metadata so it can be returned with search results but not embedded. Syncing embeddings for Column B is unnecessary and disabling sync for Column A would cause on-the-fly recomputation, increasing latency and cost.

questions.question_text: In Databricks Vector Search, what is the primary benefit of enabling **Sync Computed Embeddings** for a text column used to build the vector index (e.g., Column A)?
options: It stores the computed embedding vectors in the index so future queries can reuse them without recomputing.
It disables vector search and forces keyword-only search for faster results.
It stores only the raw text in the index and computes embeddings only at query time for better freshness.
It automatically converts all metadata columns (e.g., Column B) into embedding vectors as well.
questions.explanation: With Sync Computed Embeddings enabled, embeddings are computed once during indexing and stored in the vector index. Subsequent searches can use these stored vectors directly, avoiding repeated embedding computation at query time. The other options describe behaviors that are not the purpose of sync (disabling vector search, computing only at query time, or embedding metadata columns automatically).

questions.question_text:
Consider the following (simplified) index configuration goal:
- Column A: text used for semantic search (must be embedded)
- Column B: human-readable title to show in results (string)

A teammate proposes embedding Column B as well (i.e., treating it as another embedding column) to ensure it is returned in results.

Which response best explains why this is unnecessary and what the correct approach is?
options: A. It is unnecessary because any non-embedded column is automatically returned by vector search; no configuration is needed.

B. It is unnecessary because Column B can be stored as synced metadata (columns to sync) and returned with results; embedding it adds extra computation/storage without improving retrieval of that field.
C. It is necessary because Databricks Vector Search can only return fields that are embedded; metadata cannot be returned.
D. It is necessary because embeddings are required for all columns to maintain referential integrity between Column A and Column B.
questions.explanation: Only the field used for similarity matching needs to be embedded (Column A). Fields you want to display/return (Column B) should be included in "columns to sync" so they are stored as metadata and returned in the search response. Embedding Column B does not help return it; it just increases compute and index size.

questions.question_text:
You configured a Databricks Vector Search index with Column A as the embedding column. You also want the vector search results to include Column B (a string label) without running an additional Databricks SQL query.

What must you do during index setup and why does it avoid a separate SQL query?
questions.correct_answer: Add Column B to the index's "columns to sync" so it is stored as metadata in the vector index. During a vector search query, Databricks returns the matched records along with their synced metadata fields, so Column B is included directly in the vector search response and no separate SQL lookup is needed.
questions.explanation: Databricks Vector Search indexes store embeddings for similarity matching plus optional synced columns as metadata. When Column B is included in "columns to sync", it is persisted with the index entries and returned as part of the search results payload, eliminating the need to join back to the source table via a separate SQL query.

questions.question_text:
In a Databricks Vector Search index, you have:
- Column A: product_description (text)
- Column B: product_id (string)

You want to run semantic similarity search over product_description and return the matching product_id in the results.

Which configuration is correct?
options: A. Set Column A as the embedding column and add Column B to columns to sync (metadata).
B. Set both Column A and Column B as embedding columns so both are vectorized for retrieval.
C. Set Column B as the embedding column and add Column A to columns to sync (metadata).
D. Do not sync Column B; instead, always run a separate SQL query to fetch product_id after vector search.
questions.explanation: Vector similarity search operates on numeric vectors, so Column A (the text you search semantically) should be the embedding column. Column B does not need to be embedded; it should be synced as metadata via "columns to sync" so it can be returned alongside search results without extra embedding computation.

questions.question_text:
Consider the query below:

```sql
SELECT t1.id, t2.*
FROM table1 t1
JOIN table2 t2
ON t1.id = t2.id;
```

Assume `table2` also has a column named `id`. What is the most accurate statement about the result schema in Databricks SQL?
options: A. The query will fail because selecting `t1.id` and `t2.*` would produce two columns named `id` (a duplicate column name).
B. The query will succeed, and Databricks SQL will automatically rename one of the duplicate `id` columns to keep names unique.
C. The query will succeed, and the output will contain exactly one `id` column (Databricks SQL deduplicates join keys automatically).
D. The query will fail because `t2.*` is not allowed in a JOIN; `*` can only be used without table qualifiers.
questions.explanation: Selecting `t1.id` and `t2.*` includes `t2.id` as part of `t2.*`, which can produce duplicate column names (`id` appears twice). In many SQL engines (including Spark SQL/Databricks SQL behavior in common scenarios), duplicate output column names can cause an analysis error or downstream ambiguity. A robust solution is to exclude or alias one of the `id` columns (e.g., select `t1.id AS id` and explicitly list `t2` columns excluding `id`, or alias `t2.id AS t2_id`). Option D is incorrect because `t2.*` is valid. Options B and C describe behaviors that are not reliably guaranteed.

questions.question_text:
You are given two tables:
- `table1` aliased as `t1` with columns: `id`, `col1`, `col2`, `col3`
- `table2` aliased as `t2` with columns: `id`, `colA`, `colB`, `colC`

Write a Databricks SQL query that returns only `t1.col1` and `t1.col2` from `table1`, and all columns from `table2`, joining on matching `id` values.
questions.correct_answer:
```sql
SELECT t1.col1, t1.col2, t2.*
FROM table1 t1
JOIN table2 t2
  ON t1.id = t2.id;
```
questions.explanation: The query explicitly lists the desired columns from `t1` and uses `t2.*` to include all columns from `table2` only. The join condition `t1.id = t2.id` ensures rows are matched correctly between the tables.

questions.question_text:
In Databricks SQL, you join two tables and want to select specific columns from the first table and all columns from the second table. Which query correctly does this?

```sql
-- table1 aliased as t1, table2 aliased as t2
```
options: A. SELECT t1.col1, t1.col2, t2.* FROM table1 t1 JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;
B. SELECT t1.col1, t1.col2, * FROM table1 t1 JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;
C. SELECT t1.col1, t1.col2, table2.* FROM table1 t1 JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;
D. SELECT t1.col1, t1.col2, t2.all FROM table1 t1 JOIN table2 t2 ON t1.id = t2.id;
questions.explanation: To select all columns from only one table in a join, you qualify the star with that table’s alias (or name), e.g., `t2.*`. Option B selects all columns from the entire joined result (both tables), not just table2. Option C uses `table2.*` but `table2` is not the alias in the FROM clause (the alias is `t2`), so it would fail unless the table was not aliased. Option D uses invalid syntax (`t2.all`).

questions.question_text:
Databricks SQL fuzzy matching sometimes needs to handle cases like word reordering, partial matches, or ignoring punctuation (e.g., matching `"Fall 2024 DACC"` to `"DACC Fall 2024"`, or matching `"DACC Fall"` to `"DACC Fall 2024"`).

Explain why `LEVENSHTEIN` alone can be insufficient for these cases, and describe an approach in Databricks to implement more advanced fuzzy matching (mentioning UDFs and an example library/algorithm).
questions.correct_answer:
LEVENSHTEIN measures character-level edit distance on the full strings, so it is sensitive to token order and can penalize reordering or partial overlaps heavily (e.g., swapping words can require many edits even though the tokens are the same). It also does not inherently handle tokenization/normalization (case, punctuation) unless you pre-process.

A common Databricks approach is to implement advanced fuzzy matching via a UDF (Python/Scala) that normalizes strings (lowercasing, removing punctuation, tokenizing) and then computes a token-based similarity such as token set ratio or partial ratio using a library like `fuzzywuzzy` or `rapidfuzz`. The UDF can be applied in Spark/Databricks to score candidate pairs and filter by a similarity threshold.
questions.explanation: The key limitation is that LEVENSHTEIN is a simple edit-distance metric: it works well for small typos but not for semantic similarity patterns like token reordering or partial matches. Token-based ratios (token set/partial ratio) explicitly account for overlap in tokens and can be more robust. In Databricks, advanced logic typically comes from integrating Python/Scala libraries and exposing them through UDFs so the similarity computation can be used in queries or Spark transformations.

questions.question_text:
You want to return rows whose `opportunity_name` is "close" to the string `'DACC Fall 2024'` using edit distance. Which query correctly filters to names within an edit distance of 5?

A.
```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE LEVENSHTEIN(opportunity_name, 'DACC Fall 2024') < 5;
```

B.
```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE opportunity_name LIKE '%DACC Fall 2024%';
```

C.
```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE LEVENSHTEIN('DACC Fall 2024', opportunity_name) > 5;
```

D.
```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE opportunity_name = LEVENSHTEIN(opportunity_name, 'DACC Fall 2024');
```
options:
```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE LEVENSHTEIN(opportunity_name, 'DACC Fall 2024') < 5;
```

```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE opportunity_name LIKE '%DACC Fall 2024%';
```

```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE LEVENSHTEIN('DACC Fall 2024', opportunity_name) > 5;
```

```sql
SELECT *
FROM pathahead.motimatic_ecue.raw_stats_table_with_nextsteps
WHERE opportunity_name = LEVENSHTEIN(opportunity_name, 'DACC Fall 2024');
```
questions.explanation: LEVENSHTEIN returns an edit distance where smaller values mean more similar strings. Filtering with `< 5` (or `<= 5` depending on your threshold policy) correctly keeps strings within that distance. `LIKE` does substring matching, not fuzzy matching. Option C inverts the condition (`> 5` would keep dissimilar strings). Option D compares a string column to a numeric distance, which is invalid logic.

questions.question_text:
Which statement best describes fuzzy matching support in Databricks SQL?

A. Databricks SQL has a native FUZZY_MATCH operator for approximate string comparisons.
B. Databricks SQL has no native fuzzy matching operator, but you can approximate matching with functions like LEVENSHTEIN.
C. Databricks SQL can only do fuzzy matching through JOIN hints (e.g., BROADCAST) without any string functions.
D. Databricks SQL supports fuzzy matching only when the data is stored in Delta tables, not in other formats.
options: Databricks SQL has a native FUZZY_MATCH operator for approximate string comparisons.
Databricks SQL has no native fuzzy matching operator, but you can approximate matching with functions like LEVENSHTEIN.
Databricks SQL can only do fuzzy matching through JOIN hints (e.g., BROADCAST) without any string functions.
Databricks SQL supports fuzzy matching only when the data is stored in Delta tables, not in other formats.
questions.explanation: Databricks SQL does not provide a dedicated fuzzy matching operator. Approximate string matching is commonly implemented using built-in functions such as LEVENSHTEIN (edit distance). The other options describe features that do not exist (a FUZZY_MATCH operator), are unrelated to fuzzy matching (join hints), or incorrectly tie fuzzy matching to a storage format (Delta).

questions.question_text: A teammate claims: "To change an existing chart from a histogram to a bar chart in Databricks, you need to move the query into a notebook." Based on the provided source content, what is the best response?
options: They are correct; only notebooks allow changing visualization types.
They are correct; the SQL editor only supports histograms, not bar charts.
They are incorrect; the SQL editor supports multiple visualization types and you can add/edit charts from the visualization tab using the Visualization Editor.
They are incorrect; but the only way is to re-run the query as a dashboard tile, not in the SQL editor.
questions.explanation: The source states that the Databricks SQL editor supports bar charts, histograms, and other visualization types directly. It also explains that you can add or edit a chart via the downward-pointing arrow in the visualization tab and configure it in the Visualization Editor—so there is no requirement to move to a notebook just to change visualization types.

questions.question_text:
You ran a query in the Databricks SQL editor that returns two columns: `lead_quality_bucket` and `bucket_count`. You want a bar chart where `lead_quality_bucket` is on the X axis and `bucket_count` is on the Y axis.

What steps in the UI would you take to create/configure this chart (include the specific UI element you click to add/edit the visualization)?
questions.correct_answer: After the query runs in the Databricks SQL editor, open the Visualization tab, click the downward-pointing arrow (dropdown) to add or edit a chart, then use the Visualization Editor to select a bar chart and set X axis = lead_quality_bucket and Y axis = bucket_count.
questions.explanation: The workflow described is: run the query in the SQL editor, then use the dropdown (downward arrow) in the visualization tab to add/edit a chart, and configure it in the Visualization Editor by assigning the correct fields to the X and Y axes.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes how to create a bar chart from query results in Databricks without using a notebook?
options: You must use a notebook because the SQL editor cannot create visualizations.
Run the query in the Databricks SQL editor, then use the visualization tab and Visualization Editor to add/configure a bar chart.
Export the query results to a CSV file and create the bar chart in a third-party BI tool.
Only histograms are supported in the SQL editor; bar charts require a notebook.
questions.explanation: The Databricks SQL editor supports bar charts, histograms, and other visualization types directly. After running a query, you can use the visualization tab and the Visualization Editor to add or edit a chart—no notebook is required.

questions.question_text:
You see an error in a project that contains this line:

```python
from databricks import sql
```

The error is:

```
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'databricks.sql'
```

Which action is the most appropriate fix based on how the connector is packaged?
options: Change the import to `import databricks-sql-connector as sql` because Python imports must match the PyPI package name.
Install the PyPI package `databricks-sql-connector` (e.g., `pip install databricks-sql-connector`) so that `databricks.sql` becomes available to import.
Install a package named `dbsql` because `dbsql` is the real module name used by the connector.
Replace the import with `from databricks_sql_connector import sql` because the connector’s top-level namespace is `databricks_sql_connector`.
questions.explanation: The distribution name you install is `databricks-sql-connector`, but the import path you use in code is `databricks.sql`. A `ModuleNotFoundError` for `databricks.sql` typically means the connector package is not installed in the environment (or the wrong environment is being used). Installing `databricks-sql-connector` provides the `databricks.sql` module.

questions.question_text:
Consider the following code:

```python
from databricks import sql as dbsql
```

What does `dbsql` represent in this context, and where does the imported code come from?
questions.correct_answer: `dbsql` is a local alias for the `databricks.sql` submodule. The code being imported is provided by the installed `databricks-sql-connector` package (the pip distribution).
questions.explanation: In Python, `as dbsql` creates a local alias for the imported module. Here, `sql` is a submodule under the `databricks` namespace package, so `dbsql` refers to `databricks.sql`. That module’s implementation comes from the `databricks-sql-connector` package you installed via pip.

questions.question_text: In the Databricks Python SQL connector ecosystem, which statement best describes the relationship between `databricks-sql-connector` and `databricks.sql`?
options: `databricks-sql-connector` is a separate client library from `databricks.sql`; they implement different APIs.
`databricks-sql-connector` is the pip-installable package, and `databricks.sql` is the Python namespace (module) it provides.
`databricks.sql` is the package you install with pip, and `databricks-sql-connector` is the import path used in code.
`databricks-sql-connector` is only a CLI tool; `databricks.sql` is the only Python library.
questions.explanation: `databricks-sql-connector` is the name of the library on PyPI that you install (e.g., in `requirements.txt`). After installation, you import and use it via the Python namespace it provides: `databricks.sql`. They are two names for the same connector (distribution name vs. import name).

questions.question_text:
You want to deploy a structured Python project to Databricks Jobs rather than uploading a single script. Describe how packaging as a Python wheel can provide a cleaner entry point than a class inside a script, and outline the key steps to run it as a Databricks job.

Your answer should mention:
- where the entry point is defined
- what artifact you build/upload
- how the Jobs API runs it (at a high level)
- why this avoids relying on Databricks to "detect" a class entry point
questions.correct_answer: Package the project as a Python wheel (.whl) and define an executable entry point in packaging metadata (e.g., in setup.py/pyproject via console_scripts). Build the wheel, upload it to DBFS or a repo, install it as a library for the job, and configure the job to execute the wheel entry point (e.g., via a wheel task or by calling the installed console script/module). This is cleaner because the runtime uses the declared entry point to start execution, rather than expecting Databricks to infer a class-based entry point; the entry point explicitly maps to a callable (function/method) in your package.
questions.explanation: Databricks does not natively treat a Python class as a job entry point. A wheel-based approach moves the entry point into the packaging layer (e.g., `entry_points={"console_scripts": [...]}`), which is the standard Python mechanism for defining how a program starts. The job then installs the wheel and runs the declared entry point, making execution deterministic and easier to version, test, and deploy than uploading ad-hoc scripts that must include `if __name__ == "__main__"` logic.

questions.question_text:
You are creating a Databricks job using the Jobs API to run a Python script stored at `dbfs:/FileStore/main_script.py`. Which task type and field should you use in the job payload to run this script directly?

Assume you are using a new cluster.
options: Use `notebook_task` with `notebook_path: "dbfs:/FileStore/main_script.py"`
Use `spark_python_task` with `python_file: "dbfs:/FileStore/main_script.py"`
Use `spark_jar_task` with `main_class_name: "main_script.Main"`
Use `python_wheel_task` with `package_name: "main_script"` and `entry_point: "Main"` (without building a wheel)
questions.explanation: To run a standalone Python file from DBFS, the Jobs API uses `spark_python_task` and the `python_file` field pointing to the DBFS path. `notebook_task` is for notebooks (workspace paths), `spark_jar_task` is for JVM jars, and `python_wheel_task` requires an actual wheel artifact and declared entry point.

options: Add `if __name__ == "__main__": Main().run()` at the bottom of the file
Rename the class to `__main__` so Databricks detects it automatically
Set `notebook_task.notebook_path` to the DBFS path of the Python file
Add the class name to the job JSON payload as `"main_class": "Main"`
questions.explanation: When Databricks runs a Python file via `spark_python_task`, it executes the file as a script. Databricks does not automatically instantiate classes. Adding a standard Python entry point block (`if __name__ == "__main__"`) ensures that when the file is executed, it explicitly creates `Main()` and calls `run()`.

questions.question_text:
A production job fails with `ModuleNotFoundError` even though the notebook contains `sys.path.insert(0, "./src")`. In dev, `print(os.getcwd())` shows:

```
/Workspace/Users/alice@company.com/ecue_ingest
```

But in production, the notebook is launched from a different location and `os.getcwd()` is not the repo root. Explain:
1) Why Option 2 (`sys.path.insert(0, "./src")`) becomes fragile in this scenario.
2) Two concrete changes you could make to ensure imports resolve reliably in production.
questions.correct_answer:
1) It becomes fragile because `"./src"` is resolved relative to the current working directory. If the job is not executed with the working directory set to the repo root (e.g., not run from a Databricks Repo checkout under `/Workspace/Repos/...`), then `./src` may point to a non-existent or wrong directory, so Python cannot find the package.

2) Two reliable fixes include:
- Ensure the code is executed from a Databricks Repo (cloned under `/Repos/...`) and the job runs the notebook/script from that Repo so the working directory is the repo root.
- Stop relying on `./` and instead compute the repo root (or the notebook’s directory) and insert an absolute path to `src` into `sys.path` (e.g., derive path from `__file__` in a .py script or use a Databricks-aware method to locate the repo path).
(Alternative acceptable fix: package and install the code as a wheel/cluster library so imports do not depend on `sys.path.insert` at runtime.)
questions.explanation: Option 2 works specifically because `./src` is meaningful only when `./` points at the repo root. If a job runs a notebook from elsewhere (e.g., a user workspace path or a different working directory), then inserting `./src` adds the wrong directory to `sys.path`. Making execution consistently occur from a Repo, or using absolute/path-derived insertion (or proper packaging) removes the dependency on the working directory and makes imports robust across environments.

questions.question_text:
You have this repo structure:

```
ecue_ingest/
├── src/
│   └── ecue_ingest/
│       └── twou/
│           └── common/
```

In `notebooks/run_pipeline.py`, you add:

Which import statement is the best choice to reliably work in production when the job runs from the Repo?
options: A. `from twou.common.twou_configure_logger import _configure_logger`
B. `from src.ecue_ingest.twou.common.twou_configure_logger import _configure_logger`
C. `from ecue_ingest.twou.common.twou_configure_logger import _configure_logger`
D. `import ./src/ecue_ingest/twou/common/twou_configure_logger as logger`
questions.explanation: After inserting `./src` into `sys.path`, Python treats `src/` as an import root. The package directory under it is `ecue_ingest/`, so the correct fully qualified import starts with `ecue_ingest...`. Option A omits the top-level package, option B incorrectly includes `src` as a package name (it is a path entry, not a module), and option D is not valid Python import syntax.

questions.question_text:
In Databricks Repos, why does the following pattern typically make imports resolve correctly when running a notebook as a production job?

```python
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, "./src")

from ecue_ingest.twou.common.twou_configure_logger import _configure_logger
```
options: A. Because Databricks always adds "./src" to sys.path automatically for any notebook in the workspace
B. Because when running from a Repo, Databricks sets the working directory to the repo root, making "./src" resolve consistently
C. Because fully qualified imports do not require the package directory to be on sys.path
D. Because Git integration forces Python to treat all repo folders as installed site-packages
questions.explanation: When a notebook/job is run from a Databricks Repo, the working directory (e.g., from `os.getcwd()`) is set to the repo root. Therefore `"./src"` is resolved relative to that repo root. By inserting `./src` into `sys.path`, Python can locate the top-level package directory (e.g., `src/ecue_ingest/...`), and fully qualified imports work like a normal Python package layout.

questions.question_text:
You want strict validation in pipelines but tolerance for Databricks interactive execution where `-f` may appear. Write a Python code snippet (a few lines is enough) that:
1) Defines an `--environment` argument with choices `development|staging|production` and default `development`
2) Uses `parse_known_args()` only when `-f` is present in `sys.argv`, otherwise uses `parse_args()`
3) Logs or prints the ignored unknown arguments in the interactive case

Assume `import argparse, sys` is available.
questions.correct_answer:
```python
parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(description="Kicks off stats model build")
parser.add_argument(
    "-e", "--environment",
    choices=["development", "staging", "production"],
    default="development",
    help="Target schema environment"
)

if "-f" in sys.argv:
    args, unknown = parser.parse_known_args()
    print(f"Interactive run: ignoring unknown args: {unknown}")
else:
    args = parser.parse_args()

environment = args.environment.lower()
```
questions.explanation: The key is switching parsing behavior based on the presence of `-f` in `sys.argv`. `parse_known_args()` prevents failures caused by Databricks-injected flags during interactive runs, while `parse_args()` preserves strict validation in pipelines. Printing/logging `unknown` makes it visible what was ignored, which helps debugging without weakening pipeline correctness.

questions.question_text:
A colleague runs your script in a pipeline with strict argument validation, while you often run it interactively in Databricks where an extra `-f` argument appears. Using the snippet below, what happens in each scenario?

```python
if "-f" in sys.argv:
    args, unknown = parser.parse_known_args()
    logger.info(f"Running interactively, ignoring Databricks arguments: {unknown}")
else:
    args = parser.parse_args()
```

Choose the best description.
options: Interactive: `parse_args()` is used and will always fail; Pipeline: `parse_known_args()` is used and will accept typos silently
Interactive: `parse_known_args()` is used to tolerate extra Databricks flags; Pipeline: `parse_args()` is used to fail fast on unknown/invalid flags
Interactive: both branches behave the same because `parse_known_args()` and `parse_args()` are equivalent; Pipeline: only `-f` is parsed
Interactive: unknown args are treated as positional arguments; Pipeline: unknown args are converted to defaults
questions.explanation: The conditional is designed to detect Databricks interactive execution (presence of `-f`) and switch to `parse_known_args()` so injected arguments do not break parsing. In pipeline runs, `-f` is absent, so `parse_args()` is used to strictly validate flags, catching typos or unexpected parameters early.

questions.question_text:
In Databricks, running a Python file interactively may inject an extra CLI argument (e.g., `-f`). Given the pattern below, what is the primary reason to use `parse_known_args()` when `"-f" in sys.argv`?

```python
import argparse, sys

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("-e", "--environment", choices=["development", "staging", "production"], default="development")

if "-f" in sys.argv:
    args, unknown = parser.parse_known_args()
else:
    args = parser.parse_args()
```
options: To automatically convert all unknown arguments into environment variables
To ignore Databricks-injected arguments (like `-f`) during interactive runs while still parsing known flags
To force `argparse` to accept only the `-f` argument and reject everything else
To make `argparse` case-insensitive for the `--environment` choices
questions.explanation: `parse_args()` errors on any unrecognized arguments. Databricks interactive execution can pass extra flags (commonly including `-f`), which would cause `parse_args()` to fail. `parse_known_args()` parses the known arguments (like `--environment`) and returns the remaining unknown arguments separately so they can be ignored/logged during interactive runs.

questions.question_text:
Consider this scenario in the Databricks Git UI:

1) You are on branch `feature-a` and make several edits to a notebook but do not commit.
2) You switch to branch `feature-b`.
3) You later switch back to `feature-a`.

Which statement best describes what may happen to your edits, and what is the most robust way to prevent data loss?

A. The edits are guaranteed to reappear when returning to `feature-a`; no action is needed.
B. The edits may be lost or not reappear due to Databricks UI branch-switch behavior; committing or stashing before switching is the most robust prevention.
C. The edits will automatically be applied to `feature-b` and then back to `feature-a`.
D. The edits will be stored in Git history even without a commit, so they will reappear later.
options: A. The edits are guaranteed to reappear when returning to `feature-a`; no action is needed.
B. The edits may be lost or not reappear due to Databricks UI branch-switch behavior; committing or stashing before switching is the most robust prevention.
C. The edits will automatically be applied to `feature-b` and then back to `feature-a`.
D. The edits will be stored in Git history even without a commit, so they will reappear later.
questions.explanation: In many Git environments, uncommitted changes often remain in the working directory and may reappear when you switch back—unless they are overwritten or explicitly discarded. However, the source content highlights that Databricks UI may treat branch switching strictly, potentially discarding uncommitted changes to ensure the workspace matches the selected branch. The robust prevention is to commit (permanent) or stash (temporary) before switching branches.

questions.question_text: You have uncommitted edits in the Databricks Git UI on branch `dev` and need to switch to branch `release` to review a hotfix without losing your work. What should you do **before** switching branches, and why?
questions.correct_answer: Commit or stash your changes before switching branches. Committing records the edits permanently in `dev`’s commit history; stashing saves them temporarily so you can switch to `release` and later reapply the changes. This avoids losing work because the Databricks UI may discard or hide uncommitted changes when switching branches to keep the workspace aligned with the selected branch.
questions.explanation: The safest practice described is to commit or stash before switching. Committing makes the changes part of the branch history and guarantees they reappear when you return to `dev`. Stashing is designed for temporary preservation across branch switches. Relying on uncommitted changes persisting is risky in Databricks UI because it may reset the visible files to the branch state, potentially losing those edits.

questions.question_text:
In the Databricks Git UI, you edit a notebook on branch `feature-x` but do **not** commit or stash. You then switch to branch `main`. What is the most accurate expectation for what you will see after switching branches?

A. Your uncommitted edits will always be visible on `main` because Git keeps them in the working directory.
B. The UI will show files as they exist in `main`, and your uncommitted edits from `feature-x` may disappear (Databricks UI may reset to branch state).
C. Databricks automatically commits your edits to `feature-x` before switching.
D. Switching branches merges `feature-x` into `main` automatically, so the edits appear on `main`.
options: A. Your uncommitted edits will always be visible on `main` because Git keeps them in the working directory.
B. The UI will show files as they exist in `main`, and your uncommitted edits from `feature-x` may disappear (Databricks UI may reset to branch state).
C. Databricks automatically commits your edits to `feature-x` before switching.
D. Switching branches merges `feature-x` into `main` automatically, so the edits appear on `main`.
questions.explanation: Branch switching updates the visible files to match the target branch’s latest commit. While classic Git often keeps uncommitted changes in the working directory across branch switches (when possible), the Databricks UI may behave more strictly and revert the workspace to the selected branch state, making uncommitted edits appear to disappear. Databricks does not auto-commit and does not auto-merge on branch switch.

questions.question_text:
You want to organize output images by environment/schema in a Databricks pipeline, ensuring the directory exists before writing. Write a Python snippet that:
1) Builds a directory path under `/dbfs/tmp/` using a variable `target_schema`
2) Creates the directory if it does not exist
3) Writes the plot to `normality_plot.png` inside that directory

Assume `df_final`, `summary_analytics_obj`, and `target_schema` are already defined.
questions.correct_answer:
```python
import os

save_dir = f"/dbfs/tmp/{target_schema}"
os.makedirs(save_dir, exist_ok=True)

summary_analytics_obj.create_normality_histogram(
    df=df_final,
    column_name="deal_stage_closed_won_stage_flag_final_rate",
    save_path=f"{save_dir}/normality_plot.png",
)
```
questions.explanation: The key is to avoid relative paths and instead write to an absolute, writable DBFS-mounted path (`/dbfs/...`). Creating the directory with `os.makedirs(..., exist_ok=True)` prevents errors when the directory is missing and makes the pipeline run idempotent across executions.

questions.question_text:
You want to fix the failing write by changing only the `save_path` to a Databricks-writable absolute path. Which option is the best replacement?

```python
summary_analytics_obj.create_normality_histogram(
    df=df_final,
    column_name="deal_stage_closed_won_stage_flag_final_rate",
    save_path=____,
)
```
options: A. "plots/normality_plot.png"
B. "./plots/normality_plot.png"
C. "/dbfs/tmp/normality_plot.png"
D. "dbfs:/tmp/normality_plot.png"
questions.explanation: From Python code on the cluster, the local filesystem mount point for DBFS is `/dbfs/...`, which is writable and resolves as an absolute path. While `dbfs:/...` is a valid DBFS URI in some contexts (e.g., Spark APIs), the source content specifically indicates using `/dbfs/tmp/...` for Python file writes in this scenario.

questions.question_text:
In a Databricks pipeline, the following code fails to write the image file:

```python
summary_analytics_obj.create_normality_histogram(
    df=df_final,
    column_name="deal_stage_closed_won_stage_flag_final_rate",
    save_path="plots/normality_plot.png",
)
```

What is the most likely reason?
options: A. Databricks pipelines do not allow writing any files from Python code
B. The path is relative, and the pipeline execution working directory does not support writing files using relative paths
C. The file extension .png is not supported in DBFS
D. The dataframe df_final must be cached before any file can be written
questions.explanation: In Databricks pipeline execution, relying on a process working directory and writing to a relative path like `plots/normality_plot.png` is not supported/reliable. You must use a writable absolute path (commonly under `/dbfs/...`) so the cluster can resolve and write the file deterministically.

questions.question_text:
A production Delta table was created without column mapping mode. Multiple downstream jobs and reports depend on the column name `order_id`. You want to change the column name to `transaction_id` with minimal disruption.

Explain:
1) Why enabling column mapping mode (especially ID mapping) helps with this rename.
2) One limitation or operational consideration you must account for when adopting column mapping mode on an existing table.

(Answer in 4–8 sentences.)
questions.correct_answer: 1) With column mapping mode using internal column IDs, Delta tracks the column by a stable identifier rather than only by its external name. Renaming `order_id` to `transaction_id` changes the displayed name while keeping the underlying column identity consistent, reducing the risk of breaking pipelines that rely on the column’s underlying data. 2) A key consideration is compatibility and migration: older Delta Lake/Databricks runtimes may not support column mapping mode, and if the table wasn’t created with it, enabling it may require a migration/conversion process that can be more complex and must be planned/tested to avoid production impact.
questions.explanation: The core mechanism is that column mapping introduces internal identifiers (column IDs) so schema changes like renames/reorders don’t require rewriting data or reinterpreting which physical column holds which values. However, the content highlights limitations: not all environments support it (version compatibility), and enabling it after table creation may require migration steps that are operationally non-trivial.

questions.question_text:
You need to enable column mapping on an existing Delta table so that future renames and reorders are handled more safely. Which SQL statement correctly sets the required table property?

```sql
-- Choose the correct statement
```
options: ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping.mode' = 'id');
ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping.enabled' = true);
ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping.mode' = 'enabled');
ALTER TABLE table_name SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping' = 'id');
questions.explanation: Delta column mapping mode is configured via the table property key `delta.columnMapping.mode`, and valid values include `name` or `id` (per the provided content). Option A uses the correct key and a valid value. The other options use incorrect property keys or invalid values.

questions.question_text: In Delta Lake on Databricks, what is the primary purpose of enabling **column mapping mode** on a Delta table?
options: To store all columns in alphabetical order to improve query performance
To allow columns to be renamed without breaking existing pipelines by tracking columns with internal identifiers
To automatically encrypt column names and values at rest
To prevent any schema evolution operations such as add/drop/rename
questions.explanation: Column mapping mode enables safer schema evolution—especially renames—by decoupling the user-visible column name from how the column is tracked internally. With column mapping (notably ID-based mapping), Delta can track columns using internal identifiers so a rename changes the external name without changing the underlying column identity, reducing the risk of breaking dependencies.

questions.question_text:
A teammate using Windows says they ran `databricks configure` for a Google SSO workspace and then committed their project to Git. Later, you discover they accidentally committed the CLI config file with a PAT.

Describe:
- Where the Databricks CLI configuration file is typically located on Windows.
- Why committing it is risky.
- Two concrete remediation steps (one immediate action and one prevention measure) to reduce impact and avoid recurrence.

Answer in plain text.
questions.correct_answer:
Location: C:\Users\<username>\.databrickscfg.
Risk: It contains a Personal Access Token (PAT) which can be used to authenticate to the Databricks workspace; anyone with the token can potentially access resources permitted to that user until the token expires/revoked.
Remediation: Immediately revoke/rotate the exposed PAT in Databricks (User Settings → Access Tokens) and remove the secret from the Git history/repository. Prevention: Add .databrickscfg to .gitignore (and educate the team / enforce secret scanning) and ensure file permissions/handling practices prevent accidental sharing.
questions.explanation: On Windows the CLI stores configuration in the user profile directory as `.databrickscfg`. The file includes sensitive credentials (PATs). If committed, it may leak access to the workspace. Effective response includes revoking the compromised token (so it can no longer be used) and eliminating the leaked secret from the repository/history. Preventing recurrence requires tooling/process (e.g., `.gitignore`, secret scanning) to stop tokens/config files from being committed again.

questions.question_text:
You have configured two Databricks workspaces using the CLI. The file `~/.databrickscfg` contains:

```ini
[DEFAULT]
host = https://dbc-aaa.cloud.databricks.com
token = token_default

[prod]
host = https://dbc-bbb.cloud.databricks.com
token = token_prod
```

1) Which workspace does the CLI use if you run a command without specifying a profile?
2) How would you update the configuration so that only your user can read the file on Linux/macOS?

Answer in plain text.
questions.correct_answer:
1) It uses the [DEFAULT] profile (host https://dbc-aaa.cloud.databricks.com).
2) Run: chmod 600 ~/.databrickscfg
questions.explanation: By default, Databricks CLI reads credentials from `~/.databrickscfg`. If no profile is specified, it uses the `[DEFAULT]` section. Because the file contains sensitive tokens, restricting permissions with `chmod 600` ensures only the file owner can read/write it on Unix-like systems.

questions.question_text: When running `databricks configure` and your workspace uses Google SSO, what should you enter when the CLI prompts for a password?
options: Your Google account password
A Databricks Personal Access Token (PAT)
Your Google OAuth refresh token copied from the browser
Leave it blank; SSO will be detected automatically
questions.explanation: Databricks CLI does not directly authenticate via Google SSO. Instead, you generate a Databricks Personal Access Token (PAT) in the workspace (User Settings → Access Tokens) and paste that token when the CLI asks for a password. Databricks does not use or store your Google password for CLI authentication.

questions.question_text:
Explain the difference between (1) logging the best model run to MLflow and (2) registering a model in the MLflow Model Registry in the context of Databricks AutoML. Then describe at least two ways you could register the best AutoML model.

Your answer should address why an AutoML run can be visible in an experiment but not appear as a registered model.
questions.correct_answer:
Logging the best model run to MLflow means AutoML records an MLflow run in an experiment, capturing metrics, parameters, and artifacts (including the model artifact) for reproducibility and comparison. Registering a model in the MLflow Model Registry is a separate lifecycle step that creates (or updates) a named registered model and a version entry, enabling governance and stage transitions (e.g., Staging/Production).

In Databricks AutoML, the default behavior is to log the best run but not automatically register it, so you can see the run in the experiment without seeing a corresponding registered model.

Two ways to register the best AutoML model are: (1) use the MLflow UI to select the best run’s model artifact and choose “Register Model,” and (2) use the MLflow API (e.g., mlflow.register_model with the run’s model URI such as runs:/<run_id>/model) to create a registered model/version programmatically.
questions.explanation: This tests understanding of the separation of concerns: experiment tracking (runs/artifacts) vs model lifecycle management (registered model names, versions, stages). AutoML’s default is tracking-only; registration must be explicitly performed via UI or API, which explains why the experiment contains the run but the registry does not contain a model entry until you register it.

questions.question_text: You ran Databricks AutoML and can see the best run in the MLflow experiment, but the model does not appear in the MLflow Model Registry. What is the most appropriate next step to make it appear in the registry?
options: A. Re-run AutoML with a larger cluster because registration requires additional compute
B. Manually register the model from the MLflow UI or by using the MLflow API
C. Delete the experiment and re-run AutoML; registry entries are created only on the second run
D. Convert the model artifact to a Delta table; registry entries are created from Delta tables
questions.explanation: AutoML tracking and Model Registry promotion are distinct. By default, AutoML logs the run to MLflow but does not promote/register it. To have it show up in the Model Registry, you must manually register it via the MLflow UI or programmatically via the MLflow API.

questions.question_text: In Databricks AutoML, what happens by default to the best model produced by an AutoML run?
options: A. It is logged to MLflow (run, metrics, parameters, artifacts) but is not automatically registered in the MLflow Model Registry
B. It is automatically registered as a new model in the MLflow Model Registry and transitioned to Production
C. It is saved only as a notebook artifact and is not tracked in MLflow unless manually enabled
D. It is exported to an external model store (for example, S3) and removed from MLflow tracking
questions.explanation: The standard/default behavior is that Databricks AutoML logs the best run to MLflow (including parameters, metrics, and artifacts), but it does not automatically register the model in the MLflow Model Registry. Registration is a separate step.

questions.question_text:
A Databricks team currently hardcodes a plaintext API key in the first cell of a notebook for development. They now want to reduce the risk of accidental disclosure when using Git/version control.

Provide **two concrete actions** they should take based on the cautions in the source content, and briefly explain how each action reduces risk.
questions.correct_answer: Two appropriate actions are: (1) Do not commit notebooks that contain plaintext keys to version control (e.g., remove the key before committing, keep the key out of tracked files, or otherwise ensure it is not pushed to Git). This reduces the risk of the key being exposed to anyone with repository access or via history. (2) Transition from plaintext keys to secure secrets management (e.g., Databricks Secrets) as soon as possible for production. This reduces risk by avoiding hardcoded secrets in notebooks and centralizing secret storage/access control rather than embedding credentials in code.
questions.explanation: The source content explicitly cautions: (a) do not commit notebooks with plaintext keys, and (b) use plaintext keys only for development and move to secure secrets management for production. These actions address the main exposure vectors: repository leakage and long-term use of insecure credential handling.

questions.question_text: You are making a direct HTTP request to the OpenAI API from a Databricks notebook using `requests`. Which snippet correctly uses the plaintext key in the `Authorization` header as shown in the source content?
options:
A.
```python
headers = {"Authorization": openai_api_key_plaintext}
```

B.
```python
headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {openai_api_key_plaintext}"}
```

C.
```python
headers = {"Authorization": f"Token {openai_api_key_plaintext}", "Content-Type": "application/json"}
```

D.
```python
headers = {"Authentication": f"Bearer {openai_api_key_plaintext}"}
```
questions.explanation: The example uses an `Authorization` header with the value `Bearer <key>`. Option B matches that exact pattern. A omits the required `Bearer` prefix, C uses `Token` instead of `Bearer`, and D uses the wrong header name (`Authentication` instead of `Authorization`).

questions.question_text:
In a Databricks notebook using **Option 1 (Plaintext API Key)**, what is the recommended placement for defining the key to keep the workflow organized?

```python
openai_api_key_plaintext = "your_openai_api_key"
```
options: A. In the last cell, after all API calls, to avoid accidental use
B. In the first (setup/configuration) cell of the notebook
C. Only inline inside every API call so it is never stored in a variable
D. In a markdown cell so it is not executed
questions.explanation: The source content recommends placing the plaintext key definition in the first cell where setup/configuration occurs. This makes the notebook easier to maintain because later cells can reuse the configured variable consistently.

questions.question_text: A data scientist says: “We can create a separate Databricks serving endpoint for every experiment because endpoint creation is free, so it won’t affect our bill.” Which response is MOST accurate?
options: Correct—since creation is free, running many endpoints has no cost impact.
Mostly correct—endpoints only cost money when they receive requests, so idle endpoints are free.
Incorrect—each endpoint can start incurring per-second DBU and cloud VM charges as soon as its serving cluster is active, so many endpoints can increase cost even without invocations.
Incorrect—Databricks charges a per-endpoint monthly license fee regardless of whether the endpoint is active.
questions.explanation: While there is no one-time endpoint creation fee, each endpoint is backed by a serving cluster. Once that cluster is running, DBUs and underlying cloud VM costs accrue (often per second), even if no requests are sent. Spinning up many active endpoints can therefore increase costs; reusing an endpoint or tearing down idle ones reduces billable compute time.

questions.question_text: Your team creates a Databricks serving endpoint but does not send any requests to it for several hours. Which action most directly prevents ongoing charges during that idle period, and why?
questions.correct_answer: Configure an idle-timeout/autosuspend (or otherwise shut down/scale down the serving cluster) so the endpoint’s serving cluster stops running when unused. Even with zero invocations, DBUs (and cloud VM costs) accrue as long as the serving cluster is up; suspending it stops those compute charges.
questions.explanation: The key concept is that billing is tied to compute uptime, not request volume. If the serving cluster remains active, DBUs are billed (typically per second) regardless of whether the endpoint is invoked. An idle-timeout/autosuspend reduces billable time by turning off compute when idle.

questions.question_text: In Databricks Model Serving, which statement best describes the cost of creating a serving endpoint?
options: Databricks charges a one-time endpoint creation/setup fee, and compute is free afterward.
Creating an endpoint is free; you pay only for the compute (DBUs and underlying cloud VM costs) while the endpoint is running.
Creating an endpoint is free; you pay only when the endpoint receives requests (invocations).
You pay only for storage associated with the endpoint; compute and DBUs are not billed for serving endpoints.
questions.explanation: Databricks does not charge a one-time license/setup fee just to create an endpoint. Costs come from the compute that backs the endpoint (DBUs for the serving cluster SKU plus underlying cloud VM/storage costs) while the endpoint is running.

questions.question_text:
A team reports this scenario:

1) Developer A creates branch `feature/x` in Databricks Git folders and works for a while.
2) Developer A never clicks **Commit & Push**.
3) Separately, someone deletes `feature/x` on the remote repository (or it never existed remotely).
4) Developer A still sees `feature/x` in the Databricks Git folder days later.

Explain why this can happen in Databricks, and describe the key implication for CI/CD or collaboration when a branch has not been pushed.
questions.correct_answer: This can happen because Databricks Git folders maintain branches locally; creating a branch in the UI creates a local branch reference first. A local branch can persist in the Git folder even if the corresponding remote branch is deleted (or never existed), and Databricks can keep that local branch around for up to ~30 days. The implication is that CI/CD and other collaborators cannot use or build from that branch until it is pushed: the remote system (and pipelines triggered from the remote) only see branches/changes after the developer performs a commit and clicks Commit & Push.
questions.explanation: The evidence given is that local branches can remain for up to 30 days after a remote branch is deleted, confirming a separation between local and remote branch state. CI/CD typically runs from the remote repository; if the branch isn’t pushed, it effectively doesn’t exist for collaboration and automation.

questions.question_text:
You create a new branch in a Databricks Git folder from `main`, make no file changes, and then check your remote Git provider (e.g., GitHub/Azure DevOps). The new branch is not visible remotely.

What is the best explanation and the correct next action to make the branch appear on the remote?
options: A. Databricks branches are remote-only; wait for a background sync job to publish it automatically
B. The branch exists only locally until you create at least one commit and click **Commit & Push**; make a change (or an empty commit if supported) and push
C. The branch name is invalid; rename it to match `feature/*` and it will appear remotely without pushing
D. The branch is already remote but hidden by permissions; request admin access to view it
questions.explanation: Databricks creates the branch locally first. Remote repositories only learn about a branch when a commit is pushed that updates the remote refs. Therefore, you must commit something and then use **Commit & Push** to publish the branch to the remote.

questions.question_text: In Databricks Git folders, what happens immediately after you click **Create Branch** and enter a new branch name (before any commit)?
options: A. The branch is created both locally and on the remote repository immediately
B. The branch is created only locally in the Databricks Git folder; it is pushed to remote only after **Commit & Push**
C. The branch is created only on the remote repository; the local copy appears after you pull
D. Databricks creates a tag on the remote repository rather than a branch
questions.explanation: Creating a branch in the Databricks Git folders UI creates a local branch first. The branch is not created on the remote repository until you perform **Commit & Push**, which pushes commits (and thereby the branch reference) to the remote.

questions.question_text:
A pipeline writes new rows into the Delta table `my_table` daily (including new embeddings in the `embedding` column). You notice vector search results do not include newly added rows even though the table query shows them.

Write the SQL command(s) you should run to ensure the vector search index reflects the latest data, and briefly explain why this step is necessary.
questions.correct_answer:
Run:
```sql
ALTER VECTOR INDEX my_vector_index REFRESH;
```
This is necessary because when the underlying Delta table changes (new/updated rows), the vector search index needs to be refreshed so it rebuilds/updates its internal ANN (HNSW) structures to include the latest embeddings; otherwise similarity queries may not reflect recent data changes.
questions.explanation: The source content states that if you update the data in the Delta table, you must refresh the index using `ALTER VECTOR INDEX ... REFRESH` so the search index reflects the latest data. Without refreshing, the index can be stale relative to the table contents, leading to missing newly inserted embeddings in search results.

questions.question_text:
You have a Delta table `my_table` with an `embedding` column containing arrays like `[0.1, 0.2, 0.3]`. You create a vector index with `OPTIONS ('dim' = '4')`. When you attempt to query using:

```sql
SELECT id
FROM my_table
ORDER BY embedding <=> ARRAY(0.15, 0.25, 0.35)
LIMIT 5;
```

What is the most likely issue and the best fix?

A. The query vector must be a `VECTOR` type, not an `ARRAY`; fix by casting the query vector to `VECTOR`.
B. The index dimension does not match the actual embedding length; fix by recreating the index with `dim = '3'` (or storing 4D embeddings).
C. HNSW does not support the `<=>` operator; fix by using `COSINE_SIMILARITY()` instead.
D. `ORDER BY` cannot be used with vector indexes; fix by filtering with `WHERE embedding <=> ... < threshold`.
options: A
B
C
D
questions.explanation: B is correct: `dim` must match the dimensionality (length) of the vectors stored in the `embedding` column and the query vector. If the table has 3-element embeddings but the index is created with `dim = '4'`, the index definition is inconsistent with the data/query. The fix is to recreate the index with the correct dimension (3) or generate/store 4D embeddings consistently. A is not supported by the provided source content (the examples use `ARRAY(...)` directly). C is incorrect because `<=>` is explicitly shown as the similarity search operator. D is incorrect because the example demonstrates `ORDER BY embedding <=> ... LIMIT`.

questions.question_text:
In Databricks, which SQL statement correctly creates a vector search index on a Delta table column named `embedding` using the HNSW algorithm with 3-dimensional vectors?

A.
```sql
CREATE VECTOR INDEX my_vector_index
ON my_table (embedding)
USING HNSW
OPTIONS ('dim' = '3');
```
B.
```sql
CREATE INDEX my_vector_index
ON my_table (embedding)
USING HNSW
OPTIONS ('dim' = '3');
```
C.
```sql
CREATE VECTOR INDEX my_vector_index
ON my_table (embedding)
USING IVF
OPTIONS ('dim' = '3');
```
D.
```sql
CREATE VECTOR INDEX my_vector_index
ON my_table (embedding)
USING HNSW
OPTIONS ('dims' = '3');
```
options: A
B
C
D
questions.explanation: A is correct because Databricks vector indexes are created with `CREATE VECTOR INDEX ... USING HNSW` and the dimensionality is provided via `OPTIONS ('dim' = '3')`. B is incorrect because it uses `CREATE INDEX` (not a vector index). C is incorrect because the source content specifies HNSW. D is incorrect because the option key must be `dim`, not `dims`.

questions.question_text:
Explain **why** Parquet can improve both **storage efficiency** and **query performance** in Databricks when analysts frequently select only a subset of columns. Your answer should mention at least:
- how Parquet stores data
- how that affects what data gets read during queries
- why that impacts compression and speed
questions.correct_answer: Parquet is a columnar file format, meaning values for each column are stored together rather than storing full rows together. Because columns are grouped, Parquet can apply more effective compression and encoding (similar values are adjacent), improving storage efficiency. For queries that select only a few columns, the engine can read only the relevant column chunks/row groups instead of scanning all columns in every row, reducing I/O and speeding up execution. This selective column reading plus better compression typically yields faster queries and smaller storage footprint, especially on large datasets.
questions.explanation: A correct explanation ties Parquet’s columnar layout to two outcomes: (1) better compression due to similar data being stored contiguously within a column, and (2) faster queries because only needed columns are read from disk (less I/O), which is especially beneficial when selecting a subset of columns on large tables.

questions.question_text:
You want to create a new table in Databricks in **Parquet** format using PySpark, starting from an existing table named `sales.orders`. Which code snippet correctly does this?

A.
```python
df = spark.table("sales.orders")
df.write.format("parquet").saveAsTable("sales.orders_parquet")
```
B.
```python
df = spark.read.format("parquet").table("sales.orders")
df.write.saveAsTable("sales.orders_parquet")
```
C.
```python
df = spark.table("sales.orders")
df.write.saveAsTable("sales.orders_parquet", format="parquet")
```
D.
```python
df = spark.table("sales.orders")
df.write.format("delta").saveAsTable("sales.orders_parquet")
```
options: A
B
C
D
questions.explanation: A correctly loads the existing table into a DataFrame via `spark.table(...)` and then writes it as a managed table using `df.write.format("parquet").saveAsTable(...)`. B is incorrect because `spark.read.format(...).table(...)` is not the typical/valid pattern (you would use `spark.table` for tables, or `spark.read.format(...).load(...)` for files). C is incorrect because `saveAsTable` does not take a `format=` keyword argument in that way; the format is specified via `.format(...)`. D writes in Delta format, not Parquet.

questions.question_text:
Which SQL statement correctly creates a new Databricks table stored in **Parquet** format and populates it from an existing table?

A.
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_new_table
USING PARQUET
AS SELECT * FROM my_schema.my_existing_table;
```
B.
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_new_table
STORED AS PARQUET
FROM my_schema.my_existing_table;
```
C.
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_new_table
USING DELTA
AS SELECT * FROM my_schema.my_existing_table;
```
D.
```sql
CREATE TABLE my_schema.my_new_table
AS PARQUET
SELECT * FROM my_schema.my_existing_table;
```
options: A
B
C
D
questions.explanation: Option A uses the correct Databricks SQL CTAS pattern: `CREATE TABLE ... USING PARQUET AS SELECT ...`. This both creates the table and writes its data in Parquet format. B uses non-Databricks syntax (`STORED AS` / `FROM` in this form). C would create a Delta table, not Parquet. D is invalid syntax (the `USING` clause is required to specify the format in this style).

questions.question_text:
In Databricks, you created a local branch from the UI and expect it to track a remote branch, but you suspect tracking (upstream) is not set correctly.

Describe how you would:
1) verify in the Databricks UI whether the local branch is tracking a remote branch, and
2) what you would do if you need to manually set or fix the upstream branch (include an example Git command).

Answer in 4–8 sentences.
questions.correct_answer: 1) Open the Git menu for the linked repo and go to Branches; the UI indicates whether the currently checked-out local branch is tracking a remote branch. 2) If the upstream is missing or incorrect and the UI cannot set it, use the Databricks CLI or a terminal in Databricks to set the upstream, e.g. `git branch --set-upstream-to=origin/feature/x feature/x` (or, after checking out the branch, `git push -u origin feature/x` to establish upstream on first push). After setting it, re-check Git > Branches to confirm tracking is now shown correctly.
questions.explanation: The source flow explicitly states that tracking status can be verified in **Git > Branches**, where Databricks displays whether a local branch is tracking a remote branch. It also notes that if you need to manually set the upstream, you should use the Databricks CLI or a terminal to run Git commands; `git branch --set-upstream-to` (or `git push -u`) are standard Git ways to define the tracking relationship.

questions.question_text:
You linked a Git repository in Databricks, but the branch you ultimately want to work on (e.g., `feature/x`) is not available locally yet. According to the described UI workflow, what is the best sequence to end up with a local branch that tracks `origin/feature/x`?

Choose the most appropriate option.
options: Link the repo using any available branch (e.g., `main`), then Git > Branches, select `origin/feature/x`, and click Create local branch
Link the repo and immediately use Git > Push to create `feature/x` locally and remotely at the same time
Skip linking; configure Git Integration only, then Databricks will list all branches in the workspace automatically
Link the repo to `main`, then rename `main` to `feature/x` to make it track the remote branch
questions.explanation: If the desired branch isn’t present locally during linking, you can link using a default branch (like `main`) and then create a local branch from the remote branch via **Git > Branches > select remote branch > Create local branch**. Pushing doesn’t create a local branch from a remote tracking branch by itself; Git Integration alone doesn’t link a specific repo into a workspace folder; renaming `main` is not the correct way to establish tracking for a different remote branch.

questions.question_text: In the Databricks Git UI, after linking a repo, what action creates a **new local branch that tracks an existing remote branch**?
options: Open the Git menu, choose Pull, and Databricks will create a tracking branch automatically
Open the Git menu, choose Branches, select the remote branch, then click Create local branch
Open User Settings > Git Integration, and set the upstream branch from there
Clone the repository again and type the remote branch name as the destination folder
questions.explanation: Databricks UI supports creating a local tracking branch via the Git menu: Git > Branches, select the remote branch, then choose **Create local branch**. Pull only syncs an existing checked-out branch; User Settings configures provider authentication, not branch upstream; recloning is unnecessary and doesn’t define tracking in the intended UI flow.

questions.question_text:
You run the following query in Databricks SQL:

```sql
SELECT
  lead_quality_bucket,
  COUNT(*) AS leads
FROM leads
GROUP BY lead_quality_bucket
ORDER BY lead_quality_bucket;
```

You build a bar chart with X axis = `lead_quality_bucket` and Y axis = `leads`, but all bars appear the same color. Describe the configuration changes you would make in the Databricks visualization editor to (1) give each bucket its own color and (2) optionally assign specific colors per bucket.
questions.correct_answer: In the visualization editor, set the chart's "Group by" field to the same column as the X axis (`lead_quality_bucket`) so each bucket is treated as a separate series and each bar gets a distinct color. Then, if specific colors are required, open the "Colors" tab in the visualization editor and override the auto-assigned colors by assigning custom colors to each bucket/series.
questions.explanation: Databricks color assignment in bar charts is driven by series, not by individual rows. Setting Group by to `lead_quality_bucket` turns each bucket into its own series, which results in different colors per bar. The "Colors" tab is where you override the default palette to enforce specific colors for each series/bucket.

questions.question_text: You created a bar chart in the Databricks SQL editor where each `lead_quality_bucket` bar has a different (auto-assigned) color by setting Group by = `lead_quality_bucket`. Now you need to set specific brand colors for each bucket. What should you do?
options: A. Modify the SQL query to return a HEX color column and Databricks will automatically use it
B. Open the visualization editor and use the "Colors" tab to override the automatic palette
C. Add a WHERE clause to filter buckets one at a time and export separate charts
D. Switch the visualization type to a pie chart, because bar charts cannot have custom colors
questions.explanation: After using Group by to create separate series (and thus separate bar colors), Databricks allows you to override the automatically chosen colors in the visualization editor under the "Colors" tab. Databricks SQL visualizations do not typically map a returned HEX column directly to bar colors, and filtering/exporting or changing chart type is unnecessary.

questions.question_text: In Databricks SQL visualization, you have a bar chart with X axis = `lead_quality_bucket` and Y axis = `count(*)`. You want each bar to have a different color (one color per bucket). Which chart editor setting is the key to achieve this?
options: A. Set the "Group by" field to the same column as the X axis (e.g., `lead_quality_bucket`)
B. Set the Y axis aggregation from COUNT to SUM
C. Enable "Stacked" mode so each bar is split into segments
D. Add an ORDER BY clause to the SQL query on `lead_quality_bucket`
questions.explanation: Databricks assigns colors by series. By setting "Group by" to the same column as the X axis (`lead_quality_bucket`), each bucket becomes its own series, so each bar gets a distinct color. Changing aggregation, stacking, or ordering does not create separate series for coloring.

questions.question_text:
A Databricks job runs a long-running standalone script that imports a module and then loops for hours:

```python
# main.py
import time
from helpers import transform

for i in range(10**9):
    y = transform(i)
    if i % 1_000_000 == 0:
        print(i, y)
    time.sleep(0.01)
```

Mid-run, you edit `helpers.py` to fix a bug in `transform`. The running job continues producing outputs consistent with the old buggy logic.

Explain (1) why the running job does not pick up your edits, and (2) give two concrete ways to ensure the fixed code is used going forward (without relying on "it will eventually update").
questions.correct_answer:
1) The job does not pick up edits because Python reads and compiles imported modules into memory when they are imported (and the script itself is read/compiled up front). After `from helpers import transform` executes, the running process uses the already-loaded function object; editing `helpers.py` on disk does not change the in-memory code.

2) Two ways to ensure the fix is used:
- Stop/terminate the running job and re-run it (or restart the cluster/job run) so Python re-reads and re-imports `helpers.py`.
- Modify the code to explicitly reload the module and rebind the function (e.g., `import importlib, helpers; importlib.reload(helpers); transform = helpers.transform`) at a controlled point in the loop, understanding this must be coded intentionally and may have side effects.
(Other acceptable answers: package/version the code and deploy a new job run pointing to the new artifact; restructure into notebook cells and re-execute the updated cell/code path.)
questions.explanation: The key concept is that `.py` scripts/modules are loaded into the interpreter process (read/compiled) and executed from memory. On-disk edits mid-execution do not mutate already-imported objects. To use new code, you must either restart/re-run so imports happen again, or intentionally implement a reload/rebinding/deployment mechanism that causes the interpreter to load the updated code and use it.

questions.question_text:
You have a Databricks notebook that runs a helper module:

```python
# notebook cell
import helpers
print(helpers.transform(10))
```

`helpers.py` initially contains:

```python
def transform(x):
    return x + 1
```

You run the cell and get `11`. Without restarting the cluster, you edit `helpers.py` to:

```python
def transform(x):
    return x + 100
```

Then you run the same notebook cell again and still get `11`. What is the most likely reason?

A. Python already imported `helpers` and is using the cached module object; the file edit does not automatically update the in-memory module.
B. Databricks blocks edits to workspace files while a notebook is attached to a cluster.
C. `%run` must be used for imports to reflect changes; `import` cannot load `.py` files.
D. The change is ignored because Python only recompiles files when their size changes, not when their contents change.
options: A. Python already imported `helpers` and is using the cached module object; the file edit does not automatically update the in-memory module.
B. Databricks blocks edits to workspace files while a notebook is attached to a cluster.
C. `%run` must be used for imports to reflect changes; `import` cannot load `.py` files.
D. The change is ignored because Python only recompiles files when their size changes, not when their contents change.
questions.explanation: Python imports a module once per interpreter session and keeps it in `sys.modules`. Subsequent `import helpers` statements typically reuse the already-loaded module object rather than re-reading the file. Therefore, editing `helpers.py` on disk does not automatically affect the already-imported code in memory; you must restart the interpreter, re-run in a fresh context, or explicitly reload the module (e.g., with `importlib.reload`). This aligns with the general rule that `.py` code is read/compiled before execution and not dynamically re-fetched line-by-line.

questions.question_text:
In Databricks, you run a Python file with `%run my_script.py`. While it is still running, you edit `my_script.py` in the workspace and change a function body. What will happen in the currently running execution?

A. The running process will automatically pick up the file changes the next time the function is called.
B. The running process will not see the edits; you must stop and re-run (or re-import) to pick them up.
C. Only syntax changes are ignored; logic changes are picked up immediately.
D. Databricks always re-reads the file from disk before executing each line, so changes apply immediately.
options: A. The running process will automatically pick up the file changes the next time the function is called.
B. The running process will not see the edits; you must stop and re-run (or re-import) to pick them up.
C. Only syntax changes are ignored; logic changes are picked up immediately.
D. Databricks always re-reads the file from disk before executing each line, so changes apply immediately.
questions.explanation: When a standalone `.py` script is run (including via Databricks `%run` or as a job), Python reads/compiles the file (and imported modules) into memory and then executes that compiled code. Edits made on disk after the code has been read/compiled are not reflected in the current interpreter process. To see changes, you must re-run the script or re-import/reload the module in a fresh or updated interpreter state.

questions.question_text:
You start **Run All** on a Databricks notebook. Cell 4 is currently running. You realize Cells 6 and 7 contain a bug that would affect results.

Describe a safe procedure to (1) ensure the current run does not produce a partially-updated, non-reproducible result set, and (2) re-run with the fixes applied. Your answer should explicitly reference which edits would affect the current run vs the next run.
questions.correct_answer: To avoid a partially-updated run, stop/cancel the current Run All execution, then make the fixes to Cells 6 and 7, and then start Run All again from the top (or from a known clean point after resetting state). Edits to cells that have already executed (Cells 1–3) or the currently running cell (Cell 4) will not affect the current run because their code has already been sent to the driver. Edits to not-yet-executed cells (Cells 5–7) would be picked up if you let the current run continue, which would make the run a mix of old and new code; therefore stopping and restarting ensures the run is a consistent snapshot.
questions.explanation: During **Run All**, each cell is submitted sequentially to the driver. Changes made after a cell has been submitted (already executed or currently running) do not affect that run, but changes made to cells that have not yet been submitted will affect the remainder of that run. If you fix downstream cells mid-run and allow execution to continue, you risk producing results that combine earlier outputs from old code with later outputs from new code. Stopping and restarting after edits is the safest way to restore reproducibility and consistency.

questions.question_text:
Consider this notebook run using **Run All**:

- Cell 1 starts running and takes ~60 seconds.
- While Cell 1 is still running, you edit Cell 3.
- After Cell 1 finishes, Cell 2 runs quickly.
- Then Cell 3 runs.

Which statement best describes what code Cell 3 will run in this same **Run All** execution?
options: Cell 3 will always run the old code because **Run All** snapshots the entire notebook at the start.
Cell 3 will run the new code only if you also re-run Cell 1 after editing Cell 3.
Cell 3 will run the new code, because it had not yet been submitted to the driver when you edited it.
Cell 3 will not run at all because editing during execution invalidates the run.
questions.explanation: Databricks does not take a full-notebook snapshot for **Run All**. It submits cells one-by-one. Since Cell 3 had not executed yet (and thus had not been sent to the driver), the updated text in Cell 3 will be what gets submitted and executed when the run reaches it.

questions.question_text: In a Databricks notebook, you click **Run All**. While the notebook is running, you edit the code in a cell that has **not yet executed**. What happens when execution reaches that cell in the current run?
options: The cell executes the original code that was present when **Run All** was clicked.
The cell executes the updated code, as long as the edit happened before the cell was sent to the driver.
The cell is skipped because it was modified during execution.
Databricks restarts the driver automatically to ensure reproducibility.
questions.explanation: With **Run All**, Databricks submits cells to the active driver sequentially. A cell uses whatever text is in it at the moment it is sent to the driver. Therefore, edits made before the cell begins executing (i.e., before it is submitted) are picked up in the current run.

questions.question_text: A Delta table `existing_table(column1, column2)` is already in production. You now need an auto-incrementing `id` column to act as a primary-key-like identifier. Since you cannot add a true primary key to an existing Delta table, describe a safe migration approach to achieve this using a new Delta table with an identity column. Include the key SQL steps and explain why this approach is needed.
questions.correct_answer:
Create a new Delta table that includes an identity/auto-increment column, backfill data from the old table, then swap tables (drop/rename) to preserve the original name. Example steps:

1) Create the new table with an identity column:
```sql
CREATE TABLE new_table (
```
